concepts.title: Pressure Wave
concepts.conceptBody:
Sound as Pressure Wave 

A longitudinal wave in a fluid is described either in terms of the longitudinal displacements suffered by the particles of the medium or in terms of the excess pressure generated due to the compression or rarefaction.

Consider a wave going in the x-direction in a fluid. Suppose that at a time t, the particle at the undisturbed position $\text{x}$ suffers a displacement $\text{y}$ in the $\text{x}$-direction.

 

$\text{y}={{\text{y}}_{0}}\text{sin}\omega \left( \text{t}-\frac{\text{x}}{\text{v}} \right)$ … (i)

A is cross-sectional area.

Increase in volume of this element at time $t$ is

$\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ V}$$~=\text{A }d\text{y}$

$~=\text{A}{{\text{y}}_{0}}\left( \frac{-\omega }{\text{v}} \right)\text{cos}\omega \left( \text{t}-\frac{\text{x}}{\text{v}} \right)\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ x}$

where $\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ }y$ has been obtained by differentiating equation (i) with respect to $t$.

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ volume strain is $\frac{\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ }V}{V}=-\frac{A{{y}_{0}}\omega \text{cos}\omega \left( t-\frac{x}{v} \right)\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ }x}{vA\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ }x}=\frac{{{y}_{0}}\omega }{v}\text{cos}\omega \left( t-\frac{x}{v} \right)=\frac{\delta y}{\delta x}$

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ volume strain $=\frac{\delta \text{y}}{\delta \text{x}}$

The corresponding stress i.e., the excess pressure developed in the element at $x$, at time $t$ is

$\text{p}=\text{B}\left( \frac{-\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ V}}{\text{V}} \right)\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ where }\!\!~\!\!\text{ B }\!\!~\!\!\text{ is }\!\!~\!\!\text{ the }\!\!~\!\!\text{ bulk }\!\!~\!\!\text{ modulus }\!\!~\!\!\text{ of }\!\!~\!\!\text{ the }\!\!~\!\!\text{ material}\text{. }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ p}=\frac{\text{B}{{\text{y}}_{0}}\omega }{\text{v}}\text{cos}\omega \left( \text{t}-\frac{\text{x}}{\text{v}} \right)$ … (ii)

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }P=-\frac{B\delta y}{\delta x}$

Comparing equations (i) and (ii), the relation between the pressure amplitude ${{\text{P}}_{0}}$ and the displacement amplitude ${{\text{s}}_{0}}$ is

${{\text{p}}_{0}}=\frac{\text{B}\omega }{\text{v}}{{\text{y}}_{0}}=\text{Bk}{{\text{y}}_{0}}\Rightarrow {{\text{y}}_{0}}=\frac{{{\text{p}}_{0}}\lambda }{2\pi \text{B}}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ where }\!\!~\!\!\text{ k }\!\!~\!\!\text{ is }\!\!~\!\!\text{ a }\!\!~\!\!\text{ wave }\!\!~\!\!\text{ number}\text{. }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$

As observed from equations (i) and (ii), pressure wave is 'cos $\theta $ ' type, if displacement is described as ' $\text{sin}\theta $ ' type.

Thus, the pressure-maxima occur where the displacement is zero and displacement maxima occur where the pressure is at its normal level.

concepts.title: Energy, Power and Intensity of Sound
concepts.conceptBody:
Energy, Power and Intensity of Sound

If a sound wave given by $\text{y}=\text{Asin}\left( \omega \text{t}-\text{kx} \right)$ is propagating through a medium, the particle velocity will be ${{\text{v}}_{\text{Pa}}}=\frac{\text{dy}}{\text{dt}}=\text{A}\omega \text{cos}\left( \omega \text{t}-\text{kx} \right)$

So if $\rho $ is the density of the medium, kinetic energy of the wave per unit volume will be

$=\frac{1}{2}\rho {{\left[ \frac{\text{dy}}{\text{dt}} \right]}^{2}}=\frac{1}{2}\rho {{\text{A}}^{2}}{{\omega }^{2}}\text{co}{{\text{s}}^{2}}\left( \omega \text{t}-\text{kx} \right)$

and its maximum value will be equal to energy per unit volume $\left[ \text{as}{{(\text{KE})}_{\text{max}}}={{(\text{PE})}_{\text{max}}}=\text{E} \right]$, i.e., energy density U. So,

$\text{U}=\frac{1}{2}\rho {{\text{A}}^{2}}{{\omega }^{2}}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ S }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ x}$ … (1)

So the energy associated with a volume $\text{S }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ x}$ will be $\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ E}=\text{U }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ V}=\frac{1}{2}\rho {{\text{A}}^{2}}{{\omega }^{2}}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ S }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ x}$

So, power (rate of transmission of energy) will be

$\text{P}=\frac{\Delta\text{ E}}{\Delta\text{ t}}=\frac{1}{2}\rho v{{\omega }^{2}}{{\text{A}}^{2}}\text{ S }\left[ \text{ as }\frac{\Delta\text{ x}}{\Delta\text{ t}}=\text{V} \right]$ … (3)

Now as Intensity is defined as average energy transmitted per unit normal area per sec., i.e., power per unit area, so

$I=\frac{\Delta E}{S\Delta t}=\frac{P}{S}=\frac{1}{2}\rho v{{\omega }^{2}}{{A}^{2}}$ … (4)

Further as in case of sound wave displacement amplitude is related to pressure amplitude through the relation ${{\text{p}}_{0}}=\rho \text{vA}\omega$, so

$\text{I}=\frac{1}{2}\rho v{{\omega }^{2}}{{\left[ \frac{{{\text{p}}_{0}}}{\rho \text{v}\omega } \right]}^{2}}=\frac{1}{2}\frac{\text{p}_{0}^{2}}{\rho \text{v}}$ … (5)

Eqns. (4) and (5) give intensity of sound in terms of displacement and pressure amplitude respectively and according to these for a given source and medium

$\text{I}\propto {{\text{A}}^{2}}\left( \text{ or }\text{ p}_{0}^{2} \right)$ … (6)

Note : In case of vibrating string, as $\rho S$ will represent mass per unit length m, so from Eqn. (3) the average rate of transport of energy, i.e., power transmitted by a vibrating string will be

$\text{P}=\frac{1}{2}\text{m}{{\text{v}}^{2}}{{\text{A}}^{2}}\text{ m}=\frac{\text{mass}}{\text{ length }}$ … (7)

The SI unit of intensity is $\text{W}/{{\text{m}}^{2}}$. However; as human ear responds to sound intensities over a wide range, i.e., from ${{10}^{-12}}\text{ W}/{{\text{m}}^{2}}$ to $1\text{ W}/{{\text{m}}^{2}}$, so instead of specifying intensity of sound in $\text{W}/{{\text{m}}^{2}}$, we use a logarithmic scale of intensity called the sound level defined as

$\text{SL}=10\text{log}\left[ \frac{\text{I}}{{{\text{I}}_{0}}} \right]$ … (8)

where ${{I}_{0}}$ is the threshold of human ear, i.e., ${{10}^{-12}}\text{ W}/{{\text{m}}^{2}}$. The sound level defined in this way is expressed in decibel $\left( \text{dB} \right)$. A sound of intensity ${{\text{I}}_{0}}$ has an $\text{SL}=10\text{log}\left( {{\text{I}}_{0}}/{{\text{I}}_{0}} \right)=0\text{ dB}$ while sound at the upper range of human hearing called threshold of pain has a intensity of $1\text{ W}/{{\text{m}}^{2}}$ or a SL $=10\text{log}\left( 1/{{10}^{-12}} \right)=120\text{ dB}$.

We also use $\text{dB}$ as a relative measure to compare different sounds with one another, rather than with reference intensity; as for two intensities ${{I}_{1}}$ and ${{I}_{2}}$.

$\text{S}{{\text{L}}_{1}}-\text{S}{{\text{L}}_{2}}=10\text{log}\frac{{{\text{I}}_{1}}}{{{\text{I}}_{0}}}-10\text{log}\frac{{{\text{I}}_{2}}}{{{\text{I}}_{0}}}$

Or $\text{S}{{\text{L}}_{1}}-\text{S}{{\text{L}}_{2}}=10\text{log}\frac{{{\text{I}}_{1}}}{{{\text{I}}_{2}}}$ … (9)

e.g., two sounds whose intensity ratio is 2 differ in SL by $10\text{log}2=3\text{ dB}$. Here it must be kept in mind that ratio of two intensities corresponds to difference in their sound level (and not ratio).

Note: While solving problems related to intensity of sound along with the above, also remember that:

(i) As intensity, $\text{ I}=\frac{\Delta\text{ E}}{\text{S }\Delta\text{ t}}$ while $\text{U}=\frac{\Delta\text{ E}}{\Delta\text{ V}}$

So $\frac{\text{I}}{\text{U}}=\frac{\Delta\text{ E}}{\text{S }\Delta\text{ t}}\times \frac{\text{S }\Delta\text{ L}}{\Delta\text{ E}}=\text{v}\left[ {} \right.$ as $\Delta\text{ V}=\text{S }\Delta\text{ L}$ and $\left. \frac{\Delta\text{ L}}{\Delta\text{ t}}=\text{v} \right]$

or intensity I (energy flux) = U v

$\text{ = }\text{ Energy }\text{ density }\text{ velocity }$ … (10)

(ii) With increase in distance from the source the total energy or power transmitted remains the same but intensity decreases. For an isotropic point source of power P, intensity I at a distance $\text{r}$ from it will be

 

$\text{I}=\frac{\text{P}}{\text{S}}=\frac{\text{P}}{4\pi {{\text{r}}^{2}}}\left[ \text{ as }\text{ S}=4\pi {{\text{r}}^{2}} \right]$ … (11)

Now as for a given medium and source,

$\text{I}\propto {{\text{A}}^{2}}$ … (12)

So from Eqns. (11) and (12)

$\begin{matrix} {{\text{A}}^{2}}\propto \left( 1/{{\text{r}}^{2}} \right),\text{ i}\text{.e}\text{., }\text{ for }\text{ spherical }\text{ waves } \\ \text{I}\propto \left( 1/{{\text{r}}^{2}} \right)\text{ and }\text{ A}\propto \left( 1/\text{r} \right) \\ \end{matrix}$

(iii) In case of electromagnetic waves (e.g. light or radio waves),

$\text{I}=\frac{1}{{{\mu }_{0}}}\left( \text{ EB} \right)\text{ with }\frac{\text{E}}{\text{B}}=\text{c }\text{ and }\text{ c}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{{{\mu }_{0}}{{\in }_{0}}}}$

concepts.title: Characteristics of Sound
concepts.conceptBody:
Characteristics of Sound

Sound is characterised by the following three parameters:

(a) Loudness: It is the sensation received by the ear due to intensity of sound.

$\text{L}\propto \text{logI}$

i.e. greater the amplitude of vibration, greater will be the intensity $\text{I}\left( \propto {{\text{A}}^{2}} \right)$ and so louder will be the sound as in a shout and lesser the intensity.

The unit of loudness is decibels $\left( \text{dB} \right)$ and $\text{L}=10\text{lo}{{\text{g}}_{10}}\frac{\text{I}}{{{\text{I}}_{0}}}$ (in $\text{dB}$ ) Here, ${{\text{I}}_{0}}$ is constant i.e., minimum intensity $\left( ={{10}^{-12}}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ W}/{{\text{m}}^{2}} \right)$ just double at intermediate frequencies.

 

(b) Pitch: It is the sensation received by the ear due to frequency and is the characteristic which distinguishes a shrill (or sharp) sound from a grave (or flat) sound. As pitch depends on frequency, higher the frequency higher will be the pitch and shriller will be the sound. Regarding pitch it is worth noting that:

(1) The buzzing of a bee or humming of a mosquito has high pitch but low loudness while the roar of a lion has large loudness but low pitch.

(2) Due to more harmonic usually the pitch of female voice is higher than male.

(c) Quality (or Time): It is the sensation received by the ear due to waveform. Two sounds of same intensity and frequency as shown in fig. will produce different sensation on the ear if their waveforms are different. Now as waveform depends upon overtones present, quality of sound depends on number of overtones, i.e., harmonics present and their relative intensities. The dependence of quality on phase is controversial. Regarding quality, it is worth noting that:

(1) We can recognise a person (without seeing) by listening to his sound as it has a definite quality.

(2) If same note is played on different instruments say sitar and veena at same loudness it produces different sensation on the ear due to their quality.

(3) Sometimes it becomes difficult to recognise a person by listening to his sound on telephone or tape due to poor quality of sound.
